core_claim: GreatXML is a Windows BitLocker bypass exploit released by Chaotic Eclipse that targets the recovery partition via XML files (unattend.xml and ReAgent.xml). Writing to the recovery partition requires administrator rights, so GreatXML is a post-compromise second-stage tool (persistence and data access), not an initial-access or privilege-escalation vector. Works on systems where Defender Offline Scan was used. Second BitLocker bypass from this researcher after YellowKey.

caption:
Chaotic Eclipse (Nightmare-Eclipse, MSNightmare) released GreatXML on June 11, 2026. It is a Windows BitLocker bypass that exploits the recovery partition via XML files. The researcher found it by accident and had a working exploit in four hours.

Important framing: GreatXML is a post-compromise, second-stage tool. Writing the exploit files to the recovery partition requires administrator rights (diskpart elevation). A standard user or someone with only physical access cannot plant them. An attacker who already has admin plants the files once, then every subsequent Shift+Restart from the lock screen yields an unrestricted shell with full BitLocker volume access, no recovery key needed, surviving credential rotation and OS reinstall.

The exploit chain: use diskpart (admin) to mount the recovery partition, copy unattend.xml and Recovery/WindowsRE/ReAgent.xml to its root, then reboot into Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) via Shift+Restart. WinRE loads the malicious XML config, bypasses BitLocker authentication, and spawns an unrestricted shell with full volume access.

If Defender Offline Scan was ever run on the machine, that reboot path is already primed. No TPM extraction, no cold boot.

BitLocker reports the drive as encrypted. TPM reports healthy. But the recovery partition XML parser accepts unattend.xml and ReAgent.xml without verifying they are signed. YellowKey (CVE-2026-45585) was patched this week, but GreatXML is a separate path. The recovery partition is rarely audited and WinRE boot leaves no standard event log trail.

No vendor patch exists yet. Defenders should treat GreatXML as a persistence indicator: monitor for unattend.xml writes to the recovery partition outside Windows Update context. Also disable Defender Offline Scan if not in active use, audit the recovery partition for unauthorized XML files, and lock BIOS/UEFI to prevent unauthorized WinRE boot.

The recovery partition is not a rescue zone when its XML parser trusts unsigned config. BitLocker encryption is only as strong as the WinRE boot path sitting next to it.

Do you audit recovery partition XML files or restrict WinRE boot?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[GreatXML, Chaotic Eclipse, Nightmare-Eclipse, BitLocker, recovery partition, XML bypass, WinRE, Windows Recovery Environment, Defender Offline Scan, unattend.xml, ReAgent.xml, YellowKey, CVE-2026-45585]

Slide 2 (context): a dark floating card with one edge glowing red and a glowing timestamp marker in the upper corner, representing the June 11 GreatXML release.
Slide 3 (risk): a recovery partition icon in the dark, two XML files glowing red on its surface, a WinRE reboot arrow looping back to an unlocked BitLocker volume.
Slide 4 (mechanism): a three-step chain in the dark: XML files copying to partition, Shift+Restart reboot arrow, WinRE screen spawning a SYSTEM shell, linked by red tendrils.

Slide 5 (gap): a dark floating status surface with a green healthy indicator on one side and a red warning glow on the other, representing how standard monitoring misses the recovery partition exposure.
Slide 6 (defense): split composition, left side recovery partition with locked BIOS and XML audit checkmark, right side with red XML files and WinRE boot arrow.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a recovery partition icon, half with green rescue cross, half with two red XML files and WinRE boot arrow slipping through.
Slide 8 (cta): a recovery partition audit policy screen on a clean dark end-card.

narration.text: A researcher known as Chaotic Eclipse stumbled across a BitLocker bypass while testing Defender Offline Scan, and it took them four hours from discovery to a working exploit.

This is a post-compromise, second-stage tool: planting the files requires administrator rights, because writing to the recovery partition needs diskpart with an elevated prompt, which a standard user cannot run.

An attacker who already has admin copies unattend.xml and ReAgent.xml to the recovery partition root, and every subsequent Shift+Restart from the lock screen then bypasses BitLocker and drops into an unrestricted shell.
The payoff is persistence: planted files survive credential rotation, remote access loss, and even OS reinstall, because the recovery partition sits outside the primary OS volume.
BitLocker reports the drive as encrypted and TPM reports healthy, so standard monitoring gives no warning that the recovery partition is the actual exposure.
There is no patch yet, so monitor for unattend.xml writes to the recovery partition outside Windows Update context, audit that partition regularly, and lock BIOS to restrict unauthorized WinRE boots.
Full-disk encryption is only as trustworthy as the recovery path sitting next to it on the same drive.

beats.caption: June 11 drop. 4 hours. After RoguePlanet. MSNightmare.
Offline Scan trigger. Two XML files. WinRE boot.
XML parser trust. BitLocker bypass. Unrestricted shell.
Shows green. Parser trusts unsigned. YellowKey patched. New path.
Disable Offline Scan. Audit XML. Lock BIOS. Restrict WinRE.
BitLocker equals WinRE boot path strength.
